item.title: WEAT - the play everyone is missing — 29 replies — 52.3/hr
item.content:encoded:
WEAT - the play everyone is missing

Open thread • Replies: 29 • Updated: 2026-03-10 14:01 UTC

OP

This is potentially the most consequential second-order effect from the oil crisis and almost nobody in retail trading is talking about it yet.
Oil and natural gas aren't just fuel — they're the foundational feedstock for nitrogen fertilizer.
The Haber-Bosch process converts natural gas into ammonia, which becomes urea, ammonium nitrate, and other fertilizers.
Roughly 2-3% of global energy consumption goes to fertilizer production alone. When natural gas prices doubled after Russia invaded Ukraine, fertilizer prices tripled and we got a global food price crisis.

Now consider: European natural gas has nearly doubled already in one week. Qatar - one of the world's largest LNG exporters - declared force majeure. 20% of global LNG transits Hormuz. Europe rebuilt its entire energy security strategy around LNG after cutting off Russian gas.

The third-order effect: We're entering spring planting season in the Northern Hemisphere
RIGHT NOW. Farmers make input decisions in March-April. If fertilizer prices spike 50-100% (which is beginning), farmers either plant less, use less fertilizer (lower yields), or absorb costs they can't recover. The food price inflation from this shows up in August-October harvests -
5-6 months from now.

They can release strategic oil reserves. There are no strategic fertilizer reserves. Food prices are about to be fucked, and as you can see from the chart - if you get in early, you can profit. Let retards from Reddit chase oil prices around. Go ahead and wait for them at the finish line.

You’re welcome fuckfaces

Early replies (first 3)

61957829
>>61957787
>This is potentially the most consequential second-orde
stopped reading here. i hate faggots who try to sound smart. judging by your pic, you think wheat is going to pump? youre a fucking retard.

61957846
Ukraine was also a producer of wheat thobeit

61957902
>>61957829
I’m also right. I’m sorry you get all your trading ideas from mouth breathing retards (HURR DURR LETS LONG OIL. HURR DURR LETS BUY HIMS. Wut about muh chainlink!).

Sometimes you might want to try listening to well thought out theories and intelligence for a change. Yes you fucking retard, wheat is going to asplode. But I actually prefer if you stay out; I’d rather someone like you not profit a dime off it

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61957928
>>61957846
Ukraine reduced the wheat supply. Iran is now reducing the ability to grow more wheat to replace it. Those aren’t the same thing, anon.

The 2022 Ukraine shock alone sent WEAT from $8 to $40+. That was one disruption to one input (supply of wheat itself). Now you have the original Ukraine disruption still ongoing PLUS a simultaneous disruption to the inputs needed to grow wheat everywhere else (fertilizer). It’s a compounding problem, not a competing one.

61957973
>>61957787
Or they just mine all needed fertilizers as usual.

61957990
>>61957902
You are retarded manchild. Midwit if you will.
You will never be good trader or investor.

61957997
>>61957787
All of the price action in markets now is paper pricing, retail just jumps into whatever name they think will go up, they pump it up and crash it. They don't consider 'second order effects', if it's not something they can figure out with two brain cells then they don't jump in. That or you need a jewtuber to shill it.

61958020
>>61957997
Not really tough. Things go up and down all the time, while no one cares. It's just that OP's kind retards don't have a glue what they are looking for.

61958148
>>61957829
This, and its a general phenomena. Everyone today hates the nigger trying to sound like an authoritative source and is incentivized to either ignore the sales pitch or to do the exact opposite

61958421
>>61957973
Nitrogen fertilizer is synthesized from natural gas, not mined. 33% of global fertilizer trade transits Hormuz. QatarEnergy’s urea plant is offline. Urea up 30% in a week. Spring planting is happening now. But sure, they’ll just mine it.

You don’t mine nitrogen fertilizer. The Haber-Bosch process converts natural gas into ammonia, which becomes urea. It’s an industrial chemical process that requires massive quantities of natural gas as feedstock. The gas comes from Qatar (shut down), the Gulf states (can’t ship through Hormuz), and is processed at plants in the Middle East (several offline). You can’t just dig urea out of the ground with a pickaxe.
Phosphate and potash are mined, yes; but phosphate requires sulphur to process, and 45% of global sulphur exports transit Hormuz. So even the stuff you can mine still needs inputs that are stuck in the Persian Gulf right now.
>>61957997
That’s literally my thesis; everyone is piling into oil while the fertilizer-to-food chain is the second-order play that hasn’t been fully priced.

61959325
>>61957787
Nigga I bought 150x weat Jan 27 calls months ago

61959715
>>61957902
>I’m
your op is literal ai slop nigger kys

61959724
real chads are always long grain wood stone gold
and ore. its called being an RTS realpolitik knower

61961607
>>61957787
From what im getting, fertlizer and wheat and whatever stuff like this will peak around aug-october if this closure is bad enough? what price are you estimating these are gonna reach? just curious.

61962290
Hope you’re right. I’ve been holding since I missed the top in 2022

61962395
>>61959724
lmao this is literally how I think about it. Everyone’s fighting over the oil node while the fertilizer supply line is undefended. Age of Empires taught me more about commodity chains than any econ class

>>61961607
Aug-Oct sounds about right for peak food price impact, that’s when the Northern Hemisphere harvest comes in and we find out how much yield was lost from inadequate nitrogen application this spring. Wheat futures were at $18 before the war and hit $23+ in the first week. In 2022 when it was just Ukraine disrupting wheat supply (not fertilizer inputs), WEAT went from $8 to $13 in two weeks and eventually hit $40+. This time you’ve got the original Ukraine disruption still lingering PLUS a fertilizer input shock on top. Probably talking about at least $30 on a baseline month or two war. If this runs all spring then we could easily hit $40 and keep running.

>>61962290
Your patience might finally pay off brother. The 2022 spike was one disruption to one thing (wheat exports from Black Sea). This is a disruption to the inputs needed to GROW wheat everywhere else. Different beast.

61962454
is this ETF tracking global wheat or just american produced wheat

61962467
>>61957787
I usually only buy index funds but I will spare $1k for your WEAT

61962469
I just ate wheat pasta and shit my guts out

61962539
Okay so first off there’s many different types of fertiliser. It’s not hard to switch out or change.
Second of all a lot of farm in my country anyway, have manure pits that are used and spread in the fields.

If there’s something I’m missing please tell me but they have quite a few alternatives. You can also grow stuff without it but the yield is terrible. But still greater than 0.

61962628
>>61957787
I heard on the radio that urea is so low farmers are being rationed and what they wanted to buy is cut by upto 30%. So they are rationing the farmers with how much fertiliser they can have so everyone has an equal share to production, so does thst mean food and fuel rations for the population? The talk of a stimulus that never came, we have already been to the circus, maybe bread lines are next.

61962952
>>61958421

I'm intrigued by your theory, but isn't it possible most farmers have their fertilizer already to plant for this season. I think you're onto something but the timeframe might be pretty long and it all may balance out by next planting season.

61962954
It went up really fast after Ukraine why isn’t it surging?

61963095
>>61962539
Manure is great for a backyard garden. Global agriculture runs on synthetic nitrogen produced via the Haber-Bosch process, which requires natural gas as feedstock. There is no substitute at scale. Half of the world’s food production depends on synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, full stop. You cannot replace industrial urea with cow shit for 8 billion people. And while there are different types of fertilizer, they’re all getting hit simultaneously: urea up 30%, ammonia up 16%, phosphate up 6%, sulphur up 7%. Sulphur is needed to MAKE phosphate fertilizer and 45% of global sulphur exports transit Hormuz. You can’t just ‘switch’ when the entire input chain is disrupted at the same chokepoint

>>61962628
Yup. If farmers are getting 30% less nitrogen than they planned to apply, yields drop. Not to zero, but meaningfully. A 10-15% yield reduction across the US Corn Belt ripples through global grain markets for months. And the window to apply is right now, not when the war ends, not when ships start moving again. Every week of rationing is baked into the fall harvest numbers permanently

>>61962952
Fair point and partially true. A lot of fall/winter pre-buying happened. But spring top-dress applications of nitrogen are a separate purchase cycle that’s happening right now, and that’s the part getting disrupted. Also consider that US farmers aren’t the only ones planting. India buys 40%+ of its urea from the Middle East. Three Indian plants have already cut urea output because Qatar LNG stopped flowing. Brazil’s diesel costs are spiking which hits planting economics. This is a global supply chain, not just Iowa. You’re right that the full impact takes time to show up, that’s why this is a weeks to months trade not a days trade

61963105
>>61962954
Ukraine was a direct removal of wheat exports from the market. Prices spiked immediately because traders could quantify exactly how many million tons were gone. This is an input shock, it’s upstream of the wheat itself. The market hasn’t fully priced it because the crop damage hasn’t happened yet, it shows up in September when yields come in lower than expected. That’s the opportunity. By the time it’s obvious to everyone it’ll already be priced in

>>61962454
WEAT holds three CBOT (Chicago Board of Trade) wheat futures contracts spread across different expiration months. Those are Chicago Soft Red Winter wheat contracts, which are US-listed. But here’s the key thing — wheat is a globally fungible commodity. CBOT wheat is the most liquid wheat futures market in the world and its price reflects global supply and demand, not just American conditions.
If India’s crop suffers because three of their urea plants went offline when Qatar LNG stopped flowing, that tightens global wheat supply, which pulls CBOT prices higher because importers compete for available supply from whoever has it. If European farmers under-apply nitrogen because Middle Eastern fertilizer shipments are delayed, that reduces EU production, which means countries that normally buy from Europe bid on US wheat instead, which lifts CBOT.
The global interconnection is actually what makes the thesis so powerful. You’re not betting on one country’s crop having a bad year. You’re betting on a synchronized global fertilizer shock hitting every major growing region simultaneously during the same planting window. India, Brazil, Europe, the US Corn Belt, Southeast Asia — they all depend on fertilizer inputs that transit Hormuz or are produced with gas that transits Hormuz.

61963122
>>61957829
Followed up with some negative parallelism: “it’s not just x, it’s fucken y”, like it was the script to the final season of Stranger Things. In other words, robot hands typed this post

61963256
>>61963105
Dude you are a genius, how much on Weat do I need to make it?

61963375
Why wheat and not corn? Wheat made sense when the 2 day special military option in Ukraine started, as Ukraine is a major wheat producer, but in a nitrogen starved ag business, corn is a better choice. Corn grows faster and demands more nitrogen especially in the short term.

61963392
>>61963375
Good point I’m curious to see the response

There's no magical solution. Your question is shallow and pointless without giving us context. I assume:
>you are living with your parents
>you don't have a car (and probably no license)
>you are nearing 30 or even reached it already
>you don't have a degree
>you have a high school diploma but nothing remarkable, judging from the way you ask questions expecting everything to be chewed and spoonfed to you, and from your typos
>probably have some sort of a degeneracy that is time and money consuming
It would really help if you could write down your situation and not just vaguepost like a retard.
Also, you decided to get out of NEETdom literally at the worst possible time since the covid lockdowns. Good luck with that, but here are my tips:
First and foremost, you need to unfuck yourself. You need to get used to suffer for long hours. You need to get used to a situation where there's no light at the end of the tunnel. Laziness is fucking over - you wake up and it is all suffering until one hour before sleep. There's no comfort anymore in your world. Can't deal with this? Give up already and stay a decaying corpse.
After you are sure you are resilient and ready to make it, do the following:
>stay with your parents no matter what. you will have to still leech on them for the next several years if not decade
>get a job, even if it is the most basic shit, deliver food for all I care
>get a car license if you don't have one
>get a beater reliable car - rare these days but possible
>work on a degree that will be useful in the near future, try to aim for becoming a nurse because that's a guaranteed job forever
>get a job where you can save money
When you reach this point, make another post asking what's next.
